---
title: "IPCC Tier 2 Livestock GHG Uncertainty Calculator"
subtitle: "Getting Started with the AI-Assisted Data Translator"
author: "CIAT / CGIAR Alliance — ClimateActionNetZero"
date: "May 2026"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: false
    number_sections: false
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    keep_tex: false
  word_document:
    toc: false
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{array}
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \usepackage{fancyhdr}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{hyperref}
  - \usepackage{setspace}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \definecolor{cgiargreen}{RGB}{45,106,79}
  - \definecolor{lightgreen}{RGB}{216,243,220}
  - \definecolor{darkgrey}{RGB}{60,60,60}
  - \definecolor{amber}{RGB}{180,100,0}
  - \definecolor{infoBlue}{RGB}{30,80,160}
  - \setlength{\headheight}{14pt}
  - \pagestyle{fancy}
  - \fancyhf{}
  - \fancyhead[L]{\textcolor{cgiargreen}{\small IPCC Tier 2 Livestock GHG Uncertainty Calculator}}
  - \fancyhead[R]{\textcolor{darkgrey}{\small Getting Started — AI Translator}}
  - \fancyfoot[C]{\textcolor{darkgrey}{\small \thepage}}
  - \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
  - \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{6pt}
  - \captionsetup{font=small,labelfont=bf}
  - \hypersetup{colorlinks=true, urlcolor=cgiargreen, linkcolor=cgiargreen}
geometry: "top=3cm, bottom=2.5cm, left=2.5cm, right=2.5cm"
fontsize: 11pt
linestretch: 1.15
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

\vspace{0.4cm}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{cgiargreen}{\rule{14cm}{1.5pt}}\\[6pt]
\textcolor{cgiargreen}{\large\textbf{Version 1.0 — May 2026}}\\[4pt]
\textcolor{darkgrey}{Developed under the Global Methane Hub initiative}\\[4pt]
\textcolor{cgiargreen}{\rule{14cm}{1.5pt}}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.6cm}

# A free AI assistant for translating your data into the app template

Filling the app's input template by hand can take several hours when your raw data isn't already in the right shape. The **GMH Uncertainty Translator** is a free Claude.ai assistant — loaded with the IPCC parameter catalogue, the template schema, and a library of mapping examples — that turns your own Excel or CSV files into a ready-to-upload template in roughly **10 minutes**. No installation, no payment.

## Before you start

You will need:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Your raw inventory data file(s) — Excel, CSV, or even a PDF / screenshot of a table.
  \item The filled \textbf{Pre-flight Questionnaire} (downloaded from the app's Resources tab).
  \item About 10 minutes.
\end{enumerate}

## Step-by-step

### 1. Open claude.ai and sign in (free)

Go to \textbf{\href{https://claude.ai}{claude.ai}} and click \textit{Sign up} if you don't already have an account. The free tier is enough for this task. You can sign in with Google, email, or Apple — no payment details required.

### 2. Open the Translator Project

Click the shared link:
\textbf{\href{https://claude.ai/project/019e4594-d13d-761e-a6fb-71e242eb9804}{GMH Uncertainty Translator}}

You'll land in a chat window pre-loaded with the parameter catalogue, template schema, and mapping examples. Claude already knows everything it needs to translate your data.

### 3. Paste the filled questionnaire as your first message

\textbf{This is text you paste, not a file you upload.} Open the questionnaire (\texttt{.docx}), select everything, copy, and paste it into the chat box at the bottom of the Translator window. Click send.

Claude reads the whole questionnaire, briefly summarises back what it understood about your inventory (country, year, sub-categories, MMS systems, etc.), and then asks you to attach your raw data file(s).

\noindent\textit{Why a questionnaire? Without it, Claude has to ask you six warm-up questions one-by-one before it can do anything useful. The questionnaire gives Claude all those answers up front, so the conversation can go straight to inspecting your data.}

### 4. Upload your raw data file(s)

\textbf{This is a file upload}, not text. Click the \textbf{paperclip icon} in the chat input box, select your file(s) — \texttt{.xlsx}, \texttt{.csv}, \texttt{.pdf}, or images of tables all work — and send. Claude will inspect the file, propose a column mapping, and ask you to confirm anything ambiguous.

### 5. Answer Claude's clarification questions

You'll typically see 2–5 short questions, e.g.:

\begin{itemize}
  \item ``Your column \texttt{weight} — is this body weight (current) or mature weight (adult target)?''
  \item ``Your milk yield column is in litres — should I convert to kg using $\times 1.03$, or treat 1 L = 1 kg?''
\end{itemize}

Answer in plain language. Claude will adjust and re-confirm.

### 6. Receive the filled template

When Claude is satisfied, it will produce \textbf{\texttt{filled\_template\_for\_app.xlsx}} as a downloadable file. Click the download icon next to it in the chat.

### 7. Upload into the Cattle Uncertainty App

In the app's \textbf{Data Input} tab:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Click \textit{Upload your filled template (.xlsx)}.
  \item Select \texttt{filled\_template\_for\_app.xlsx}.
\end{enumerate}

The app will validate the file and load it. Any issues appear in the \textbf{QA/QC} tab — \textcolor{cgiargreen}{green} = OK, \textcolor{amber}{amber} = review, \textcolor{red}{red} = fix.

You are now ready to run the uncertainty simulation.

\newpage

## Tips for a smooth session

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Be specific.} ``Population in column B is total dairy cattle in 2022'' beats ``B is animals''.
  \item \textbf{Tell Claude when something is wrong.} If a unit conversion looks off, just say ``wait — that should be 275 kg, not 599 kg'' and Claude will fix it.
  \item \textbf{Ask Claude to explain.} ``Why did you set Ym = 6.5?'' is a fine question — it will cite the IPCC table.
  \item \textbf{Don't worry about advanced parameters.} The 16 IPCC coefficients (\texttt{Cfi}, \texttt{Ca}, \texttt{Ym}, \texttt{EF3}, ...) ship with sensible defaults. You only need to provide them if you have country-specific measurements.
\end{itemize}

## When the AI gets it wrong

The Translator is a helper, not an oracle. Always:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Spot-check} the key numbers (population, body weight, milk yield) by comparing with your original file.
  \item \textbf{Watch the QA/QC tab} for amber and red flags after upload — those highlight values outside IPCC ranges.
  \item \textbf{Tell us} when something is off. Use the in-app Feedback tab to report mistranslations so we can improve the prompt.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{cgiargreen}{\rule{14cm}{0.6pt}}
\end{center}

\textit{Produced by the CGIAR Alliance, Climate Action–Net Zero Initiative, Project D614 — GMH Emissions Uncertainty. Questions: \href{mailto:M.Lolita@cgiar.org}{M.Lolita@cgiar.org}.}
